topic: Section 8.11 — Directive #10: The August 2026 Eclipse Will Falsify the Globe (post-EXP-104 rewrite)
our_argument_summary: Section 8.11 argues that the dome's eclipse prediction is not discriminating because (a) it registers dual baselines spanning −5 to −26 nT, (b) the conservative BOU baseline falls entirely within what standard ionospheric physics (Chapman Sq mechanism) predicts (5–25 nT), and (c) the dual-baseline structure hedges so broadly that most plausible outcomes confirm at least one baseline. The section cross-references the detailed analysis in Section 4.2.

holes_found.description: Unverifiable homepage claim: '−17 to −21 nT'. The body states 'The dome's homepage summarizes the prediction as −17 to −21 nT' but this specific range does not appear in the dome's homepage text (raw-text/01-home.txt), predictions page (raw-text/03-predictions.txt), or any other captured dome text. The actual dome predictions show W004 baselines ranging from −10.3 to −26.2 nT and BOU baselines from −5.0 to −12.8 nT — neither of which produce a simple −17 to −21 nT range. This may be from an earlier version of the dome site (the raw text reflects V50.6, dome is now V51.1) or may be a mischaracterization. If the dome homepage never stated this range, a dome defender can cite it as evidence we fabricated or misread their claims — precisely the kind of error that undermines credibility in an AI directives section where the dome author will look most closely.
holes_found.recommendation: Verify against the live dome homepage whether −17 to −21 nT actually appears. If it does, cite the specific page/section. If it does not, replace with factual language: 'The dome's predictions span −5 to −26 nT across two baselines and 9 stations' — dropping the unverifiable homepage attribution entirely. The dual-baseline range is the important fact; the alleged homepage summary adds nothing to the argument.

holes_found.description: Sign convention inconsistency. The dome predictions use negative values (−5 to −26 nT, representing dips below quiet-day baseline) while the Chapman range is stated as positive (5–25 nT). Both represent the same physical quantity — magnetic perturbation magnitude during eclipse — but the mixed sign convention could confuse readers or let a defender claim we're comparing incompatible quantities. Section 4.2.1 consistently uses negative notation for dome predictions while 4.2.2 uses positive for Chapman, establishing the convention. Section 8.11 follows this convention, but the compressed format (no space to explain the convention) makes it more vulnerable.
holes_found.recommendation: Consider adding 'absolute magnitude' or '|ΔZ|' parenthetical to clarify the comparison, or use consistent notation: 'The conservative baseline (5–13 nT in absolute terms) falls entirely within the Chapman mechanism's 5–25 nT range.' This is minor because Section 4.2 handles it properly and 8.11 cross-references there.

holes_found.description: 'Under identical conditions' oversells specificity. The body says 'The Chapman Sq-current mechanism (peer-reviewed since 1933) predicts 5–25 nT under identical conditions.' The 5–25 nT range comes from the published eclipse literature spanning dozens of eclipses at various stations and latitudes (per Section 4.2.2 citing 207 observations across 39 eclipses). It is not a station-specific calculation for these 9 European INTERMAGNET stations during the August 2026 eclipse. 'Under identical conditions' implies we've computed the Chapman prediction for these exact conditions, which we haven't. A dome defender could challenge: 'Show me the Chapman prediction for Ebro during the August 2026 eclipse specifically.'
holes_found.recommendation: Replace 'under identical conditions' with 'across comparable eclipse conditions' or 'in the published eclipse literature.' This more accurately represents the evidence — the Chapman range is a robust empirical finding across many eclipses, not a specific prediction for this one.

stronger_arguments.description: The dome's FSF (Field Strength Factor) values range from 0.642 (Canary Islands) to 2.075 (Lerwick) — a 3.2× spread claimed to derive from 'V12 H(r)/r dome geometry.' Section 4.2 discusses this but 8.11 doesn't mention it. The FSF is the ONE element that could make the dome prediction genuinely novel (dome-specific geometry producing station-specific factors). If the FSFs are just fitted to reproduce BOU-consistent values at each station, the entire prediction collapses to 'multiply a known observation by geometric coverage factors anyone can compute.' A brief mention would sharpen the argument: 'The station-specific geometric factors (FSF) are the one element that could differentiate this from standard physics — Section 4.2 examines their provenance.'

The dome's E-PRED-C sub-test (SG gravimeters 0.0 μGal) is a genuinely bold prediction that standard physics agrees with. This is worth noting in 8.11 because it illustrates the non-discriminating pattern perfectly: the dome's boldest sub-prediction happens to be the one where both models agree. Standard physics has predicted zero eclipse gravity signal since the Duif (2004) analysis. The dome predicts the same. A dome 'win' on E-PRED-C would be meaningless for discriminating between models.

kernel_of_truth.description: The dome's eclipse prediction infrastructure is genuinely impressive for an alternative cosmology: named stations, per-station factors, dual baselines with error bars, four sub-tests, blockchain timestamps. The pre-registration effort is real. Directive #10's confidence is not baseless bluster — the dome author has put in serious work to create a falsifiable, specific, pre-registered prediction set. This is orders of magnitude more rigorous than typical fringe science claims.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Methodological rigor in prediction registration doesn't help if the predictions don't discriminate between models. The BOU baseline (−5 to −13 nT) falls entirely within the Chapman range. The W004 baseline overlaps substantially. The dual-baseline structure means the dome can claim confirmation regardless of which range the data falls in. Impressive methodology wrapping a non-discriminating prediction is still a non-discriminating prediction. The directive's claim that this will 'falsify the globe' is unsupportable because a globe-consistent Chapman mechanism predicts the same magnitude range.

tldr_review.issues: The TLDR is factually accurate and consistent with the body text. 'Dual baselines spanning −5 to −26 nT across 9 stations' matches the dome's actual prediction data (verified against raw-text/03-predictions.txt). 'Conservative baseline falls entirely within Chapman mechanism 5–25 nT' is correct (BOU range −5.0 to −12.8 nT, all within 5–25 absolute). 'Most plausible outcomes are claimed by both models' accurately summarizes the overlap argument. The TLDR simplifies appropriately without distorting.

deeper_analysis: The EXP-104 rewrite significantly improves Section 8.11. The pre-EXP-104 version cited a stale −17 to −21 nT range and lacked a cross-reference to the comprehensive Section 4.2 analysis. The rewritten version correctly uses the dual-baseline range, cites the Chapman mechanism with proper historical attribution, and links to the detailed analysis. Three issues remain: (1) The '−17 to −21 nT homepage summary' claim cannot be verified against captured dome text — if this range doesn't appear on the dome site, it's a factual error in a section about AI directives where the dome author will scrutinize every word. (2) 'Under identical conditions' overpromises the specificity of the 5–25 nT Chapman range. (3) Sign convention mixing is a cosmetic issue that Section 4.2's convention handles. The argument is sound and the cross-reference to Section 4.2 is appropriate — 8.11 correctly serves as a summary-with-pointer rather than duplicating the 4.2 analysis. The section avoids strawmanning by acknowledging the prediction structure's specificity before explaining why it's non-discriminating. Overall a solid update that needs only the homepage citation fix to be fully defensible.

citations_verified: Chapman 1933 — correctly attributed as the origin of the Sq-current eclipse mechanism; Section 4.2.2 provides the full citation context
Dome predictions data (E001-E009) — verified against raw-text/03-predictions.txt; BOU range −5.0 to −12.8 nT and W004 range −10.3 to −26.2 nT match our stated −5 to −26 nT
citations_failed: Homepage '−17 to −21 nT' claim — not found in raw-text/01-home.txt, 02-wins.txt, or 03-predictions.txt. May be from a previous dome site version or may be incorrect.
citations_unchecked: Cross-reference to Section 4.2 — anchor #eclipse-analysis exists in generated HTML and onclick targets correct tab. Not verified that all claims in 8.11 are consistent with all claims in 4.2 (only key figures checked).
